message:
REVIEW: make InMemoryCowBtrfsTree::range a true B-tree descent

`InMemoryCowBtrfsTree::range(start, end)` materialised the entire tree
via `collect_all_items()` before filtering, so every call was O(N)
regardless of window size. That invalidated the complexity claim in
bd-yt66z's `btrfs_resolve_inode_path_via_cow` — the writable fast path
does one `range()` call per INODE_REF hop expecting O(log N + k) per
hop, but got O(N) per hop. For a tree with N items and depth D, the
post-refactor writable resolution cost was O(D · N), i.e., *slower*
than the single O(N) `walk_btrfs_fs_tree` the refactor replaced.

Rewritten `range` now descends the B-tree:
- Leaves: `partition_point` to locate the first item >= start, then
  break on the first item > end.
- Internal nodes: skip children whose upper separator `keys[i]` is
  <= start (entire subtree < start) and stop once a child's lower
  separator `keys[i-1]` is > end (entire remaining span > end).

Per call: O(log N + k). Total writable-mode resolution cost becomes
O(D · log N) as the commit message originally claimed.

`collect_all_items` and its `collect_from` helper are now unused and
deleted (project AGENTS.md discourages dead-code shims). Existing
coverage kept intact: `range_returns_inclusive_sorted_window` still
passes; the 1000-op `random_mutations_preserve_invariants_and_ordering`
proptest compares full-range output against a `BTreeMap` model over
every insert/update/delete step and stayed green.

Added regression test `range_descends_multi_level_tree_without_full_materialisation`
with max_items=3 and 40 keys (forcing multiple internal levels) that
covers middle-of-tree windows, below-tree windows, above-tree windows,
and point queries.

Tests: ffs-btrfs 164, ffs-core --lib 632, fmt + clippy clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
